conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children? If yes, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm interested in finding out if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! To help assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for your child, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, could you tell me your child's name and age?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: My child's name is Alex, and they're 15 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! Now I need to understand a bit more about your situation.

Do you live in the UK, and is Alex living with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK and Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Let me ask a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Are you responsible for Alex? By this I mean, do you provide for their care and upbringing (such as providing their food, clothing, and accommodation)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm responsible for Alex's care - I provide everything they need, their food, clothing, and they live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete my assessment.

Is anyone else also claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question before I can give you my assessment:

Are you or your partner's individual income over £60,000 per year? (This doesn't affect whether you can *claim* Child Benefit, but it may affect whether you need to pay a tax charge.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'd rather not get into the income details right now - I'm mainly just trying to find out if I'm actually eligible to claim it in the first place.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's absolutely fine - I have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me just read the official guidance to confirm the rules, and then I'll give you my assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for your patience. Based on the information you've provided and the official UK government guidance, I can now give you my assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on your circumstances:
- Alex is 15 years old (under 16)
- You live in the UK
- Alex lives with you
- You are responsible for Alex's care and upbringing
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

You meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. You can go ahead and make a claim.

Just to note: I mentioned income earlier because if you or a partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay a High Income Child Benefit tax charge. However, this doesn't stop you from being eligible to claim - it just means some or all of it might need to be paid back through tax. You can still claim and receive the payments regardless of income.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 15 years old, which is under 16, so they meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. You live in the UK with Alex and are responsible for their care, providing food, clothing, and accommodation. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. According to the official eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK, and you'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Alex meets all these criteria, so you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for them.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 15-year-old child Alex, is responsible for Alex's care and upbringing, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. All eligibility criteria are met for Child Benefit.
